We establish equilibrium characterizations, identify conditions under which the dynamics admit scalar Lyapunov or exact-potential representations, and analyze how switching and acceptance costs shape local stability. Numerical experiments test finite-time stabilization, cost sensitivity, and a special convex-game benchmark.

AI.tldr: The paper explores coalition formation via decentralized decision-making and examines the dynamics under the Aumann-Dreze value framework, focusing on stability and payoff allocation.
AI.motivation: The goal is to understand how decentralized, unilateral decisions drive coalition formation and how payoff distributions can be well-characterized in such settings.
AI.method: The study uses theoretical characterizations, Lyapunov and exact-potential frameworks, and numerical experiments to explore the dynamics and stability of coalition structures.
AI.result: The research establishes equilibrium conditions, identifies potential functions for dynamics, and examines the effects of switching and acceptance costs through both theoretical and numerical approaches.
AI.conclusion: Coalition structures that are stable can emerge from decentralized exit-and-join decisions under specific cost conditions, linking cooperative and noncooperative behaviors.

title: Before the Labels: How Dataset Construction Shapes Suicidality Detection in Clinical Text
comment: To appear in the Proceedings of the 11th Workshop on Computational Linguistics and Clinical Psychology (CLPsych 2026)
summary: Clinical NLP increasingly relies on electronic health record (EHR) data to detect suicidal behaviors, treating clinical documentation as more reliable ground truth than social media. We argue that this framing obscures how EHR-based suicidality datasets encode a particular operationalization of suicidality, shaped by who authors the data, how episodes are bounded, and how ambiguity is resolved. We ground this argument in a case study of the ScAN dataset, built over MIMIC-III clinical notes. We show how governance constraints, ICD-based cohort selection, single-annotator labeling, and hospital-stay-level aggregation produce labels that reflect clinician-documented judgments, treat suicidality as a bounded episode, and assume that intent can be reliably inferred from documentation. A linguistic analysis demonstrates that identical labels subsume heterogeneous clinical framings differing in temporality, negation, and uncertainty. We argue that clinical NLP should examine the assumptions embedded in suicidality datasets before interpreting their labels as ground truth.

AI.tldr: The paper critically examines how EHR-based suicidality datasets are constructed, particularly focusing on their assumptions and limitations.
AI.motivation: To highlight potential issues in using EHR data as a reliable ground truth for clinical NLP tasks in detecting suicidality, pointing out the biases and operationalizations embedded in dataset creation.
AI.method: A case study analysis of the ScAN suicidality dataset derived from MIMIC-III clinical notes, assessing factors like governance constraints, cohort selection, labeling by single annotators, and hospital-stay aggregation.
AI.result: The study finds that data labels reflect clinician judgments, treat suicidality as a contained episode, and rely on documentation to infer intent. Through linguistic analysis, it reveals inconsistencies and heterogeneous clinical interpretations under similar labels.
AI.conclusion: Researchers using suicidality datasets in clinical NLP should critically evaluate the embedded assumptions and limitations, avoiding the direct acceptance of labels as absolute ground truth.

title: CoRaCommit: A VS Code Extension for Commit Message Generation with Exemplar Retrieval
comment: 17 pages, 6 images, 3 tables, Manuscript submitted to a Journal (2026)
summary: Commit messages are essential textual artifacts that describe the intent behind code changes, and play a critical role in version control, code review, and historical tracking. However, in practice, commit messages are primarily authored manually, which is time-consuming and often results in inconsistent quality and non-uniform expression. Existing VS Code extensions for commit message generation typically directly invoke large language models based on the code diff, without leveraging similar commit exemplars as references, and rarely support user feedback-driven LLM recommendation. To address these limitations, this paper presents CoRaCommit, a VS Code extension that enhances commit message generation by retrieving similar commit exemplars as prompt context, invoking multiple LLMs in parallel for candidate commit message comparison, and dynamically recommending LLMs based on user feedback. Experimental results on 945 commits from the ApacheCM dataset show that CoRaCommit outperforms existing VS Code extensions across BLEU, CIDEr, METEOR, and ROUGE-L metrics, demonstrating the effectiveness of retrieval-augmented context for commit message generation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CoRaCommit, a VS Code extension designed to improve commit message generation using retrieval-augmented context and dynamic LLM recommendation.
AI.motivation: Manual generation of commit messages is inconsistent and time-consuming, and existing tools fail to leverage similar commit examples or adapt based on user feedback.
AI.method: CoRaCommit retrieves similar commit exemplars for context, utilizes multiple LLMs in parallel for comparisons, and dynamically adapts to user feedback in LLM recommendation.
AI.result: CoRaCommit outperforms existing tools on multiple natural language processing metrics, proven through experiments on the ApacheCM dataset.
AI.conclusion: CoRaCommit enhances commit message quality and consistency by combining retrieval-augmented context and adaptive LLM-based recommendations, outperforming traditional tools.

title: HypOProto: Hyperbolic Ordinal Prototypes for Left Ventricular Filling Pressure Classification
summary: Echocardiography (echo) is a widely used imaging modality for assessing cardiac function, with Left Ventricular Filling Pressure (LVFP) serving as a critical physiological marker for conditions such as heart failure. Standard LVFP classification into normal \emph{vs} elevated categories relies on the Doppler-derived $E/e'$ ratio, which is operator-dependent and often unavailable in resource-limited settings, motivating methods that infer LVFP directly from B-mode echo. Existing deep learning approaches achieve high performance but remain largely black-box, limiting clinical interpretability. We propose HypOProto, a hyperbolic, ordinal prototype-based framework for interpretable LVFP classification using a frozen, explainable foundation model backbone. HypOProto arranges prototypes along the physiological $E/e'$ scale, placing borderline cases near the hyperboloid root where small angular differences separate similar cases, while normal and elevated cases occupy outward positions reflecting increasing diagnostic certainty. This hyperbolic geometry encodes clinically meaningful ordinal relationships and improves interpretability. We also introduce a novel Hyperbolic Prototype Angular Separation (HyperPAS) loss, enforcing inter-class prototype separation in hyperbolic space. HypOProto achieves SOTA performance while maintaining transparency, and highlights clinically relevant regions in visualizations. This work represents the first prototype-based framework for LVFP classification in echo. Our code can be found at https://github.com/DeepRCL/HypOProto.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HypOProto, a hyperbolic prototype-based deep learning framework for classifying Left Ventricular Filling Pressure (LVFP) from echocardiography, improving interpretability and achieving state-of-the-art performance while maintaining transparency.
AI.motivation: Left Ventricular Filling Pressure (LVFP) is a critical metric in diagnosing heart conditions such as heart failure. Existing LVFP classification methods like the $E/e'$ ratio are often subjective, operator-dependent, and inaccessible in resource-limited settings, motivating the development of interpretable machine learning methods to classify LVFP directly from B-mode echocardiography.
AI.method: The authors propose HypOProto, which leverages hyperbolic geometry and a novel Hyperbolic Prototype Angular Separation (HyperPAS) loss function to achieve interpretable LVFP classification. Prototypes are arranged along the physiological $E/e'$ scale in hyperbolic space, encoding ordinal relationships and diagnostic certainty through spatial positioning.
AI.result: HypOProto achieves state-of-the-art performance in LVFP classification while ensuring clinical interpretability. The framework identifies borderline cases and visualizes clinically relevant regions, thus improving clarity for medical practitioners.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that using a hyperbolic prototype-based framework not only advances the performance of LVFP classification but also enhances clinical interpretability and transparency, making it a significant contribution to echocardiographic diagnostics.

title: Robust $Q$-learning for mean-field control under Wasserstein uncertainty in common noise
summary: In this article, we present a robust $Q$-learning algorithm for discrete-time mean-field control problems under Wasserstein uncertainty in the common noise law. The algorithm combines a quantization-and-projection scheme with a Wasserstein dual reformulation on the common-noise space. We establish its convergence together with finite-time iteration bounds for both synchronous and asynchronous learning schemes. Numerical experiments on systemic risk and epidemic models compare the asynchronous implementation with an idealized Bellman iteration, illustrate the robustness-performance tradeoff under common-noise misspecification, and report the observed convergence behavior of the asynchronous $Q$-learning algorithm.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a robust $Q$-learning algorithm for mean-field control problems under uncertainty from a common noise, achieving theoretical convergence and demonstrating effectiveness through experiments.
AI.motivation: To address robust control challenges in discrete-time mean-field problems influenced by uncertain common noise.
AI.method: The authors use a quantization-and-projection scheme combined with Wasserstein dual reformulation, and evaluate convergence for both synchronous and asynchronous learning approaches.
AI.result: The algorithm shows convergence with finite-time iteration bounds and is validated through numerical experiments on systemic risk and epidemic models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed $Q$-learning algorithm is effective and robust under common-noise uncertainty, showcasing practical performance and robustness tradeoffs in tested scenarios.

title: Safe Local Navigation for Ackermann-Steered Robots in Unmapped Environments
comment: Presented at the 23rd Conference on Robots and Vision (CRV 2026)
summary: A control framework is proposed for safe local navigation of mobile robots equipped with Ackermann steering in unmapped environments where a global goal is absent. Based on local obstacle detections, the safest heading angle is determined along the direction of the largest open space ahead of the vehicle. Guided by this direction, bounding lines are constructed on the left and right sides of the vehicle to achieve obstacle separation. These bounding lines are obtained by solving a convex quadratic optimization that maximizes vehicle-to-obstacle clearance. Optionally, conditions are imposed on the bounding lines to preserve parallelism and smooth abrupt changes from prior control steps. A feedback-linearizing controller is then used to regulate the vehicle's distance from one or both bounding lines, effectively enabling tracking of a local reference path that preserves safety through obstacle clearance maximization. Open-source code is included for the application of this control scheme. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method produces safer navigation paths with significantly shorter computation times, compared to some existing exploration-based planners.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a control framework enabling safe local navigation for mobile robots with Ackermann steering in unmapped environments, maximizing obstacle clearance and computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Enhance safety and efficiency in mobile robot navigation without a global goal in unknown terrains.
AI.method: Determines safest heading based on obstacle detection, uses bounding lines for obstacle avoidance via quadratic optimization, and regulates robot distance using a feedback-linearizing controller.
AI.result: Experimental results show safer navigation paths and shorter computation times versus current exploration-based planners.
AI.conclusion: The method improves local navigation safety with computational efficiency; open-source code provided enhances accessibility.

title: Thermodynamic Signatures of Reasoning: Free-Energy and Spectral-Form-Factor Diagnostics for Hallucination Detection in Large Language Models
summary: Hallucination detection in large language models (LLMs) is deployment-critical, and recent work shows that the spectrum of attention-derived graph Laplacians carries strong signal about reasoning quality. Prior spectral diagnostics, however, summarize the Laplacian spectrum by a handful of eigenvalues or hand-picked scalars, leaving most of its structure unused. We propose Free-Energy Signatures (Fes), a spectral descriptor that treats each layer's attention Laplacian as a Hamiltonian and extracts its thermodynamic potentials partition function, free energy, spectral entropy, heat capacity together with the random-matrix-theory (RMT) spectral form factor. We prove three results: (i)~Lipschitz stability of Fes under attention perturbation; (ii)~an expressiveness result showing that Fes enriches finite spectral summaries and approximates moment-derived spectral functionals under explicit regularity and grid-resolution assumptions; and (iii)~a finite-sample PAC bound on the AUROC of a training-free detector built from Fes. Empirically, across six open-weight LLMs and six benchmarks, a lightweight probe on Fes descriptors achieves the strongest aggregate AUROC among attention-spectral baselines, improving over LapEig by $+6.5$ AUROC points and over GoR-4 by $+2.4$ points on average, while requiring no update to the underlying LLM. In the fully unsupervised setting, an RMT-deviation score achieves mean AUROC $0.71$, providing a label-free but weaker detector. A complementary RMT analysis shows that correct generations exhibit more Wigner-Dyson like spectral statistics, whereas hallucinations exhibit more Poisson-like statistics. The anonymized code and config are provided in the supplementary material.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Free-Energy Signatures (Fes), a novel spectral descriptor for detecting hallucinations in large language models (LLMs) based on the attention-derived Laplacian spectrum.
AI.motivation: Existing attention-based spectral diagnostics for hallucination detection in LLMs underutilize the structural information contained in the Laplacian spectrum, relying mainly on a limited set of eigenvalues or scalars.
AI.method: The authors propose the Free-Energy Signatures (Fes) framework, which extracts detailed thermodynamic potentials such as partition function, free energy, spectral entropy, and others from the attention Laplacian. They also prove stability, expressiveness, and detection bounds for Fes, and perform empirical tests across multiple LLMs and benchmarks.
AI.result: The Fes framework outperforms prior attention-spectral diagnostics with a higher AUROC improvement, achieving a mean AUROC of up to 0.71 in an unsupervised setting. Additionally, the results reveal distinct spectral statistical patterns for correct generations (Wigner-Dyson) versus hallucinations (Poisson).
AI.conclusion: Free-Energy Signatures enable more precise hallucination detection in LLMs, achieving state-of-the-art AUROC performance without altering the model or requiring supervised training.

title: Beyond Static Leaderboards: Predictive Validity for the Evaluation of LLM Agents
summary: Agent benchmarks are growing fast, but no single benchmark touches more than four or five of the dimensions that deployment exposes. This paper aggregates the largest coordinated deep-dive of one MCP-based industrial-agent benchmark to date: fourteen parallel implementation studies covering new asset classes (including a multi-modal visual extension), alternative orchestrations, retrieval strategies, reasoning modes, infrastructure optimizations, and evaluation-methodology probes. Consolidating those studies with seven prior agent benchmarks, we argue that aggregate-score leaderboards systematically underspecify deployed-agent evaluation. Rankings derived from aggregate scores do not transfer to out-of-distribution settings; recent public-to-hidden competition retrospectives provide direct empirical evidence of this rank instability. We propose ranking configurations by predictive validity, the correlation between in-sample and out-of-sample rank, rather than in-sample mean, and report a twelve-tier measurement apparatus that exposes the deployment-relevant dimensions HELM and its agent-era successors collapse. The position is operationalized through three falsifiable out-of-distribution criteria with explicit thresholds; existing evidence partly supports it but is too thin to confirm. We close with a pre-registered pilot design and a field-level vision for what the next generation of agentic benchmarks should report.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques current agent benchmarks for their inability to represent real-world deployment conditions and proposes a new evaluation approach based on predictive validity, supported by a multi-faceted measurement protocol.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of current agent benchmarks, which fail to capture the variety of dimensions critical for real-world deployment and often produce unstable rankings when models are evaluated out of distribution.
AI.method: The authors aggregate results from fourteen simultaneous studies on an industrial-agent benchmark, and combine them with findings from seven other benchmarks to analyze ranking stability and propose predictive validity as an evaluation framework.
AI.result: The paper finds that aggregate-score leaderboards are insufficient for evaluating deployed agents, as rankings often fail to generalize to out-of-distribution settings. It also presents a twelve-tier measurement apparatus for better understanding deployment-relevant dimensions.
AI.conclusion: Current agent benchmarks need rethinking to focus on predictive validity, with a more rigorous, multi-dimensional evaluation approach. The authors suggest several testable criteria and outline a pilot study to support their framework.

title: MiqraBERT: Regression-Based Sentence-BERT Finetuning for Biblical Hebrew Parallel Detection
summary: Textual reuse pervades the Hebrew Bible, yet the computational methods used to detect it still rest largely on lexical overlap, and they falter once a parallel involves paraphrase, lexical substitution, or syntactic reworking. This paper introduces MiqraBERT, a Sentence-BERT model finetuned from AlephBERT (a Modern Hebrew encoder) for verse-level semantic similarity in Biblical Hebrew. The training set comprises 1,650 labeled verse and half-verse pairs: 825 true parallels drawn from the Chronicles synoptic material and from foundational studies of poetic parallelism, balanced against 825 randomly sampled negatives. Through cosine-similarity regression, the model learns an embedding space in which parallel verses cluster together and unrelated verses move apart. We evaluate separation with distribution-based metrics, Wasserstein distance and the overlap coefficient, across ten random seeds. MiqraBERT improves distributional separation 2.7-fold over the pre-trained baseline and reduces the ambiguous overlap region from roughly 24% to about 6%. Narrative synoptic parallels reach a recall@10 of 87.1%; poetic parallels remain difficult, below 9%. This genre-dependent asymmetry confines the model's reliable scope to narrative textual reuse. MiqraBERT is publicly available at https://huggingface.co/davidmsmiley/MiqraBERT

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MiqraBERT, a fine-tuned Sentence-BERT model designed to detect semantic similarities in Biblical Hebrew verses, focusing on improving textual reuse detection beyond simple lexical overlap.
AI.motivation: Current methods for detecting textual reuse in the Hebrew Bible often fail when encountering paraphrases, substitutions, or syntactic changes. A more sophisticated model is needed to effectively identify semantic connections.
AI.method: MiqraBERT is derived from AlephBERT, fine-tuned using 1,650 labeled verse pairs (825 parallels and 825 non-parallels). Using cosine similarity regression, it generates embeddings for verse-level semantic similarity and assesses performance with separation metrics such as Wasserstein distance and overlap coefficient.
AI.result: MiqraBERT demonstrates a 2.7-fold improvement in distributional separation compared to the baseline, reducing ambiguous overlaps from 24% to approximately 6%. Recall@10 for narrative parallels is 87.1%, but struggles with poetic parallels, achieving less than 9%.
AI.conclusion: While effective for narrative textual reuse, MiqraBERT has limited reliability for poetic parallels due to genre-specific challenges. The model is publicly accessible for further research and usage.

title: JAMER: Project-Level Code Framework Dataset and Benchmark on Professional Game Engines
summary: Current AI-driven game development has made substantial progress in asset generation, gameplay design, and web-based game coding, yet project-level code engineering on professional game engines remains largely unexplored due to the absence of large-scale datasets and deterministic evaluation methods. We present JamSet and JamBench, the first project-level game code framework dataset and benchmark built on a professional game engine. Our key insight is that Game Jam competitions, community events where developers build complete games under tight time constraints, yield thousands of open-source projects suitable for this purpose. Building on the Godot engine's text-based format and headless execution mode, we design a deterministic verification pipeline from file integrity to runtime behavior collection, distilling 8,133 verified projects from over 240,000 repositories. Of these, 300 manually verified projects form JamBench; the rest constitute JamSet. JamBench defines theme-driven generation and code completion tasks, evaluated through a pipeline combining compilation pass rates, Structural Completeness Score (SCS), and Behavioral Alignment Score (BAS). Evaluation of 9 frontier models reveals a capability cliff as project scale increases, with runtime pass rates dropping from 80.4% on small projects to 5.7% on large ones (Task2a). Code Agents improve compilation rates yet yield no gains in runtime behavioral quality, indicating that the bottleneck lies in architectural design rather than syntactic correctness. Experiments validate JamSet as effective training data. All data and code are publicly available.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces JamSet and JamBench, new datasets and benchmarks for AI-driven project-level game code development using the Godot engine, designed from thousands of verified open-source game projects and evaluated under a custom pipeline.
AI.motivation: The field of AI-driven game coding lacks datasets and evaluation methods for project-level code engineering on professional game engines despite progress in lower-level tasks like asset generation and simple gameplay design.
AI.method: The researchers utilized Game Jam competitions to collect over 8,000 verified game projects built on the Godot game engine. They created a deterministic pipeline for verifying these projects' file integrity and runtime behavior. JamBench, a benchmark subset of 300 manually verified projects, was established for evaluation tasks like theme-driven generation and code completion.
AI.result: Experiments with 9 state-of-the-art AI models reveal that as project scale increases, performance decreases significantly, especially in runtime pass rates. While Code Agents can improve compilation rates, they fail to enhance runtime behavioral quality, identifying architectural design as the key bottleneck.
AI.conclusion: JamSet and JamBench effectively address the need for large-scale datasets and deterministic evaluations for project-level game coding. The resources, publicly available, pave the way for testing and improving AI models in this domain and highlight challenges like architectural bottlenecks.

title: ParaScale: Scale-Calibrated Camera-Motion Transfer via a Gauge-Invariant Parallax Number
summary: Transferring the camera motion of a reference video to a freshly generated one lets creators reuse cinematic moves. Yet reference and target often live at incompatible scales -- a sweep across a galaxy versus a nudge across a desk -- and naively reusing the recovered trajectory yields either imperceptible or violently exaggerated motion. We trace this to a geometric fact: translation-induced image motion scales as ||T||/Z, so a monocular trajectory is meaningful only up to a depth-scale gauge. We distill this into the Parallax Number Pi = ||Delta T|| / Zbar, a dimensionless, gauge-invariant descriptor of how strongly a camera move is felt, and prove that it -- not the raw trajectory -- is the quantity that scale-faithful transfer must preserve. ParaScale is a plug-and-play module that reads Pi off any reference video and re-realizes it against the target scene's own depth, per frame, leaving rotation untouched. Sitting between pose extraction and pose injection, it requires no retraining and drops into any pose-conditioned generator. We further introduce the Parallax Consistency Error (PCE), a scale-symmetric metric that -- unlike the similarity-aligned TransErr -- exposes scene-scale mismatch. Across scale regimes spanning four orders of magnitude and multiple backbones, ParaScale keeps the realized parallax on the identity line and cuts PCE by more than 3x over uncalibrated transfer with no loss of visual fidelity.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "ParaScale," a module for transferring camera motion across videos of varying scales by preserving a new descriptor, the Parallax Number (Pi), instead of raw trajectories. It retains cinematic depth accuracy.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to solve the challenge of transferring camera motion between videos where the scales differ drastically, such as galaxy-wide sweeps versus small desk nudges, as current methods lead to imperceptible or extreme motion artifacts.
AI.method: They introduce "ParaScale," which uses the Parallax Number (Pi) as a depth-aware gauge-invariant descriptor for camera motion transfer, and design it to be a plug-and-play module compatible with existing pose-conditioned generators.
AI.result: The system accurately transfers camera motion across scales by preserving realized parallax, introduces a new scale-symmetric metric called Parallax Consistency Error (PCE), and significantly reduces PCE by over 3x compared to uncalibrated methods.
AI.conclusion: ParaScale effectively enables scale-faithful cinematic motion transfer across drastically different spatial scales, maintaining accuracy and visual quality without retraining for different datasets.

title: Leveraging tails for adaptation
summary: We consider contraction of Bayesian posterior distributions in nonparametric settings where coefficients of a function over a basis or dictionary are given priors with $p$--exponential tails, including Laplace tails $(p=1)$ and heavier tails $(p<1)$. It is shown that contraction rates improve as $p$ decreases and that full adaptation to smoothness, up to logarithmic factors, is obtained in an appropriate $p\to 0$ regime. As applications, we consider both series priors in white noise regression and shallow ReLU neural networks in random design regression. In particular, we show that overparametrised shallow ReLU networks can adapt to any regularity $0\le β\le 2$. Through a simulation study, we show strong empirical agreement with the behavior predicted by our theory.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the Bayesian posterior contraction rates under $p$-exponential priors, showing improved rates for smaller $p$ values and adaptiveness in specific settings.
AI.motivation: To improve understanding of how Bayesian posterior contraction rates behave under different prior tail assumptions, especially with $p$-exponential tails.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of contraction rates for $p$-exponential priors in nonparametric problems, supported by simulations in white noise regression and shallow ReLU neural networks.
AI.result: Improved contraction rates as $p$ decreases, full adaptation to smoothness in $p \to 0$ regime, and validation through applications in white noise regression and shallow ReLU networks.
AI.conclusion: Using $p$-exponential priors (particularly with smaller $p$ values) enhances Bayesian posterior contraction rates and provides adaptivity across different smoothness levels.

title: ForEnt: A Multi-Modal Dataset for Characterizing Quadruped Robot Entrapments in Forest Environments
summary: Legged robots are increasingly deployed in forests for ecological surveying and monitoring, yet their autonomy is often interrupted consequent to the challenges posed in traversing forest environments. Forest entrapments, for example, when a robot's legs are ensnared in vines or other vegetation, result in loss of stability and toppling. Such events not only disrupt the mission and require manual intervention, but also risk damage to the robot hardware. To address the absence of a dedicated dataset to investigate these failure modes in forest environments, we present ForEnt, a multi-modal dataset collected with the low-cost Unitree Go2 quadruped across eight forest sites in the Southampton Common Woodlands, UK. For our dataset, over approximately 1.7 km of traversals in 11 sequences were conducted, yielding 69 recorded entrapment events. ForEnt includes time-synchronized RGB-D images, LiDAR scans, proprioceptive data, and third-person video, enabling analysis of terrain factors contributing to entrapment and providing labeled sensor streams for reproducible benchmarking. By supporting the evaluation of entrapment detection strategies, ForEnt lowers the barrier to developing robust quadruped robot deployments in challenging forest environments.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ForEnt, a multi-modal dataset to study legged robot entrapment in forest environments, collected using the Unitree Go2 quadruped.
AI.motivation: Legged robots face autonomy failures in forests due to difficult terrains like vines or vegetation which risk stability, toppling, and hardware damage. A specialized dataset is required to study these challenges systematically.
AI.method: The researchers gathered approximately 1.7 km of traversal data from eight forest sites in Southampton Common Woodlands, UK, using the Unitree Go2 quadruped. This dataset includes RGB-D images, LiDAR scans, proprioceptive data, and third-person videos, yielding 69 recorded entrapment events.
AI.result: The ForEnt dataset provides synchronized multimodal sensor streams and labeled data, enabling thorough analysis of terrain factors and reproducible benchmarking for entrapment detection strategies.
AI.conclusion: ForEnt facilitates the development and evaluation of robust strategies for detecting and mitigating robot entrapment, advancing the deployment of legged robots in challenging forest settings.

title: FlexLAM: Resolving the Bottleneck Trade-off in Latent Action Learning
summary: Latent actions provide a compact interface between action-free video and downstream decision-making, yet existing Latent Action Models (LAMs) force every transition through a fixed-capacity bottleneck. We identify a bottleneck trade-off: overly tight codes can discard transition cues needed for action alignment, while overly loose codes preserve additional transition variation that must be resolved when alignment labels are scarce or narrowly distributed. FlexLAM replaces this fixed capacity with variable-length latent actions trained by nested dropout, yielding prefix-valid codes that capture compact transition structure first and add detail only when needed, without new architectures or losses. A single FlexLAM matches or surpasses separately trained fixed-capacity LAMs at every evaluated token budget under standard scarce-label supervision and under a low-return single-task alignment stress test, indicating that FlexLAM is not merely adjustable at inference time but learns a better latent-action interface at the same token budgets. The same model supports inference-time token-budget adjustment without retraining, and FlexLAM improves Ego4D transition reconstruction. These results suggest that variable-length latent actions are an architecture-free, drop-in upgrade to the fixed-capacity bottleneck in latent action models, latent-action world models, and video-pretrained action interfaces.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces FlexLAM, a model using variable-length latent actions trained via nested dropout, solving bottleneck trade-offs in current Latent Action Models (LAMs).
AI.motivation: Current LAMs use fixed-capacity bottlenecks that either discard essential transition cues or over-preserve unnecessary details, impacting action interface performance.
AI.method: FlexLAM uses variable-length latent representations trained by nested dropout, producing prefix-valid codes that adaptively capture transition structure without altering architectures or losses.
AI.result: FlexLAM outperforms fixed-capacity LAMs under various token budgets, enhances transition reconstruction, and allows token-budget adjustments at inference without retraining.
AI.conclusion: Variable-length latent actions, implemented via FlexLAM, provide a significant and architecture-free improvement over fixed-capacity bottlenecks in LAMs and related applications.

title: GLARE: A Natural Language Interface for Querying Global Explanations
summary: While global explanations are crucial for understanding vision models across datasets, classes, and decision contexts, their complex and monolithic nature often hinders practical exploration. Because users typically seek targeted answers to specific questions rather than static artifacts, we present an LLM-based interactive interface that provides natural language access to global explanations for black-box image classifiers. The system's core LLM acts as a mediator, translating natural language questions into structured SQL queries over local explanation data. This enables flexible aggregation without exposing users to low-level representations. For each query, the interface outputs statistics-augmented natural language responses, supporting local explanations, and intent-aligned visualizations. We evaluate the system on intent interpretation, query mapping accuracy, generalization to novel queries and datasets, and robustness to linguistic errors. Our results demonstrate that LLM-mediated querying substantially improves the accessibility and usability of global explanations for human-centered XAI.

AI.tldr: An LLM-based interactive system is developed for users to query global explanations of image classifiers using natural language, making them accessible and practical for targeted exploration.
AI.motivation: Existing global explanations for image classifiers are complex and monolithic, making it difficult for users to explore them effectively and find answers to specific questions.
AI.method: The authors proposed a system utilizing an LLM to mediate between users' natural language questions and structured SQL queries interacting with local explanation data, enabling flexible aggregation of global explanations.
AI.result: The system has been evaluated on criteria such as intent interpretation, query accuracy, generalization, and robustness to linguistic inconsistencies, showing improvements in accessibility and usability of the explanations.
AI.conclusion: Integrating LLMs into the querying of global explanations enhances human-centered explainable AI by enabling more accessible and user-tailored exploration.

title: Creating Multilingual Mental Health Dialogue Datasets: Limits of Persona-Based Localization via Nationality and Language
comment: 15 pages, 4 figures. Accepted to the 2026 Workshop on Computational Linguistics and Clinical Psychology (CLPsych 2026), co-located with ACL 2026
summary: AI and large language models (LLMs) have emerged as promising tools to address global mental health challenges. Despite the global nature of these challenges, there remains a critical shortage of high-quality datasets for training and evaluating such systems. To mitigate this gap, researchers increasingly generate synthetic clinical personas to simulate user data and test digital mental health support systems. However, most validated personas rely on English-centric contexts. This paper investigates whether similar persona-based methods can be used to generate multilingual mental health datasets. We modified nationality and language parameters in personas to generate clinical dialogues in Mandarin, Bengali, and Hindi. We then examined how different LLMs perform when evaluating the depression severity of these generated multilingual datasets against the baseline in English. Our findings indicate that just adding nationality and language parameters in personas might not be adequate, as it can introduce clinical inconsistency across languages. LLM judge models often exhibit inaccuracies in assessing depression severity in non-English texts, with performance varying across different models. This exposes the systemic limitations of applying English-centric personas to multilingual contexts. Ultimately, our work highlights the urgent need for culturally responsive data generation to ensure equitable mental health systems globally.

AI.tldr: This paper explores the use of language models to generate multilingual mental health datasets, discovering that current methods relying on English-centric clinical personas are insufficient due to inconsistencies and inaccuracies in non-English contexts.
AI.motivation: Address the shortage of high-quality datasets for training and evaluating digital mental health systems, especially in multilingual and global contexts.
AI.method: The authors modified language and nationality parameters in synthetic clinical personas to generate dialogue data in Mandarin, Bengali, and Hindi. They tested the ability of various LLMs to evaluate depression severity in these datasets, comparing performances across models and against an English baseline.
AI.result: The findings reveal that solely adjusting language and nationality parameters introduces clinical inconsistencies across languages. LLMs performed inconsistently when evaluating depression severity in non-English datasets, with results differing based on the model.
AI.conclusion: English-centric methods of generating clinical personas are insufficient for multilingual contexts, underscoring the need for culturally responsive and contextually specific data generation approaches to support equitable global mental health systems.

title: Repository-Level Solidity Code Generation with Large Language Models: From Prompting to Fine-Tuning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown strong capabilities in general-purpose code generation, but their effectiveness in specialized software domains remains underexplored. Solidity smart contracts represent a high-stakes domain where generated code must satisfy strict language-level, security, and software-engineering constraints. Existing benchmarks and metrics remain insufficient for repository-level Solidity generation, where models must synthesize complete contracts from natural language requirements. To address this gap, we introduce SolidityBench, a benchmark of 5,470 repository-level Solidity smart contracts paired with natural language descriptions. We also propose SolidityScore, a Solidity-aware semantic metric that emphasizes domain-critical constructs such as security modifiers, contract declarations, and Solidity-specific keywords. Using this benchmark, we evaluate representative code LLMs, including Qwen2.5-Coder, DeepSeek-Coder, and CodeLlama, across zero-shot prompting, Chain-of-Thought reasoning, in-context learning, retrieval-augmented generation, and supervised fine-tuning. The results show that general-purpose models exhibit systematic structural deficiencies in repository-level Solidity generation. Among non-parametric methods, retrieval-augmented generation performs best, while in-context learning degrades beyond two examples due to context saturation. Supervised fine-tuning achieves the largest improvement by internalizing Solidity-specific constraints into model parameters. Overall, our study provides a comprehensive benchmark for repository-level Solidity code generation and shows that high-quality domain data combined with supervised fine-tuning is the most effective strategy for improving the reliability of LLM-generated smart contracts.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SolidityBench, a benchmark dataset for Solidity smart contracts, and SolidityScore, a new evaluation metric. It evaluates code LLMs and finds supervised fine-tuning, combined with domain-specific data, achieves the best results for Solidity code generation.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of reliable benchmarks and metrics for evaluating repository-level Solidity smart contract generation from natural language requirements.
AI.method: Developed SolidityBench, a dataset of 5,470 Solidity smart contracts with paired natural language descriptions, and proposed SolidityScore, a domain-aware metric for evaluation. Tested various code generation approaches, including retrieval-augmented generation, in-context learning, and fine-tuning, on this dataset.
AI.result: General-purpose code LLMs exhibited structural deficiencies in generating Solidity contracts. Among techniques, retrieval-augmented generation performed best among non-parametric methods, while supervised fine-tuning achieved the most significant improvements by incorporating domain-specific constraints.
AI.conclusion: The paper highlights the importance of high-quality domain-specific data and supervised fine-tuning for improving large language models in specialized domains like Solidity smart contract generation.

title: Training-Free Metrics for Synthetic Object Detection Data: A Proxy for Detector Performance
summary: With the recent advent of image generative models, synthetic data are increasingly being used to supplement limited real datasets for training computer vision models. However, not all synthetic datasets improve performance equally, and their effectiveness can only be assessed by training a downstream model, which is computationally expensive and time-consuming. This problem is pronounced in the task of object detection, where the required annotations are much more dense due to bounding boxes. In this paper, we propose a pre-computable metric family, dubbed Conditional-Composition Domain Match (CCDM), which serves as a proxy for the relative utility of candidate synthetic training sets for downstream detection. Experiments on the VisDrone-DET dataset show that the CCDM metric families achieve a Spearman correlation of 1.0 with the downstream performance of YOLOv8, clearly outperforming existing metrics for synthetic image evaluation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Conditional-Composition Domain Match (CCDM) metric family to assess the utility of synthetic data for training object detection models, showcasing a strong correlation with performance on the VisDrone-DET dataset.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the inefficiency and high computational cost of assessing synthetic datasets for improving downstream object detection tasks, especially due to dense annotations required for bounding boxes.
AI.method: A pre-computable metric family called CCDM is introduced as a proxy metric to estimate the efficacy of synthetic training sets, replacing the need for fully training object detection models for each candidate dataset.
AI.result: The CCDM metric family achieved a perfect Spearman correlation of 1.0 with the downstream performance of YOLOv8 on the VisDrone-DET dataset, surpassing other existing metrics for evaluating synthetic images.
AI.conclusion: CCDM presents an effective and computationally efficient tool for assessing synthetic datasets' quality for object detection tasks, eliminating the need to train models repeatedly.

title: Optimal Deterministic Multicalibration and Omniprediction
summary:
A model is multicalibrated on a collection of group weights $G$ if it is calibrated -- i.e. unbiased even conditional on its prediction -- not just overall, but also after reweighting contexts by each $g \in G$. It is a useful property for many downstream applications and is a basic desideratum of trustworthy machine learning. Before this work, all predictors known to attain the minimax-optimal $\widetilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})$ sample complexity rate for $\varepsilon$-multicalibration were randomized, while deterministic predictors were known only with substantially worse sample complexity. Whether randomization is necessary for optimal sample complexity in multicalibration was explicitly asked by [CLNR26] and implicitly in several prior works.
  We resolve this open problem by giving a minimax-optimal multicalibration algorithm that outputs a deterministic predictor. We then generalize the algorithm to produce optimal deterministic predictors that satisfy outcome indistinguishability (OI) with respect to finite or finitely covered collections of tests. As an application, this also gives deterministic omnipredictors and panpredictors with optimal sample complexity, resolving open problems posed by [OKK25] and [BHHLZ25].

AI.tldr: The paper presents a deterministic algorithm for achieving optimal multicalibration, addressing an open problem about whether randomization is necessary for this task.
AI.motivation: Multicalibration ensures unbiased predictions even after reweighting contexts, and achieving it with optimal sample complexity in a deterministic manner has been an open problem.
AI.method: The researchers design and propose a deterministic multicalibration algorithm that attains minimax-optimal sample complexity ($\widetilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})$). They generalize this to create deterministic predictors satisfying outcome indistinguishability for a range of tests.
AI.result: The new algorithm resolves the need for randomization in multicalibration while maintaining optimal sample complexity. It also facilitates deterministic omnipredictors and panpredictors.
AI.conclusion: The work eliminates the necessity of randomization for optimal multicalibration and expands the scope by producing deterministic predictors suitable for broader applications.

title: Route-Constrained Robust Fusion Estimation for MEMS/GNSS Integrated Navigation of Unmanned Ground Vehicles in GNSS Degraded Environments
comment: Accepted workshop paper, 1st Workshop on Robot Meets GNSS and Ranging for Seamless Autonomy, IEEE ICRA 2026
summary: To address cumulative localization drift of unmanned ground vehicles in structured road environments under severe Global Navigation Satellite System signal occlusion, this paper proposes a robust route-constrained state estimation method. During periods without satellite signals, the proposed method establishes the correspondence between the historical dead reckoning trajectory and local segments of the mission route extracted from a high-definition map, and estimates a route-referenced position via a two-dimensional rigid transformation. The estimated position is then formulated as a pseudo-position observation and incorporated into an Extended Kalman Filter update. In this way, route constraints at the road level can be continuously injected into a unified state estimation framework, thereby suppressing position deviation relative to the mission route while indirectly improving azimuth estimation. To enhance practical applicability, engineering strategies, such as trigger control, matching quality validation, route offset compensation, and single update correction limiting, are further introduced. Experiments in three representative scenarios, including a long tunnel, a multi-segment tunnel, and a curved tunnel, show that the proposed method effectively suppresses error accumulation during satellite outages, reduces the risk of large maximum deviation, and improves localization continuity and road-level usability.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a method to reduce localization drift for unmanned ground vehicles in structured roads under poor GNSS signal conditions by using route-based state estimation and Extended Kalman Filter updates.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the problem of cumulative localization drift in unmanned ground vehicles operating in structured road environments where GNSS signals are severely occluded, like tunnels.
AI.method: The method establishes a correspondence between the historical dead reckoning trajectory and local mission route segments from a high-definition map, generating a route-referenced position using a 2D rigid transformation, and integrating this into an Extended Kalman Filter framework.
AI.result: Experiments conducted in specific scenarios such as long, multi-segmented, and curved tunnels demonstrate a reduction in error accumulation, a lowered risk of large deviations, and improved localization performance during GNSS outages.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively mitigates localization drift during GNSS outages, enhances road-level usability, and incorporates practical strategies for real-world deployment.

title: Spectral DPPs via NEPv: A Scalable Continuous Relaxation of Determinantal MAP for Diversity-Aware Data Selection
summary: Selecting a small, diverse, high-quality subset from a massive pool of candidates is a recurring primitive in modern machine learning -- data curation and coreset selection for training and fine-tuning large models, active-learning batch acquisition, prompt and exemplar selection for in-context learning, retrieval diversification, and experimental design. Determinantal Point Processes (\DPP s) give a principled, well-calibrated notion of diversity for this task, but their \emph{MAP} objective -- pick a size-$k$ subset $S$ maximizing $\logdet(L_S)$ -- is NP-hard, and the standard greedy and sampling algorithms scale superlinearly in the ground-set size $n$. This cost is prohibitive precisely in the data-centric regime where diversity matters most, where $n$ ranges over millions to billions of candidate examples, features, or embeddings. We recast \DPP-MAP as a continuous optimization problem over the Stiefel manifold, and show that its first-order optimality conditions form a \emph{Nonlinear Eigenvalue Problem with eigenvector dependency} (\NEPv) of a previously unstudied form. This \NEPv\ admits a self-consistent field (\SCF) iteration with a spectral-gap-based local contraction guarantee, giving a principled iterative solver where the diversity objective drives an eigenvector-dependent operator. The resulting algorithm, \OurMethod, requires only matrix-vector products with the kernel and runs in time $O\!\big((ndk+nk^2)\,t\big)$ for a small number of iterations $t$, scaling near-linearly in $n$ and integrating directly with low-rank and feature-map kernels common in ML. This paper focuses on the relaxation, solver, and scaling analysis; full real-data benchmarking is left to a planned empirical study.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenge of selecting diverse, high-quality subsets from large datasets, a critical task in machine learning. It proposes a novel approach to optimize Determinantal Point Processes for this purpose, making it computationally efficient for large-scale data.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to make subset selection based on diversity feasible and efficient for massive datasets, as current methods face prohibitive computational costs and scalability issues.
AI.method: The authors reformulate the DPP-MAP problem as a continuous optimization problem over the Stiefel manifold, solving it using a Nonlinear Eigenvalue Problem with eigenvector dependency (NEPv) and introducing a new algorithm called \OurMethod.
AI.result: The proposed algorithm, \OurMethod, scales near-linearly with dataset size and achieves efficient optimization by employing a self-consistent field iteration. Its computational complexity is near-linear in $n$ for practical usage.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a scalable, principled optimization method for subset selection problems in machine learning, though real-world benchmarking is reserved for future study.

title: Interpreting Neural Combinatorial Optimization via Evolving Programmatic Bottlenecks
summary: Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO) achieves strong performance, yet its black-box nature remains a key roadblock to deployment and scientific diagnosis. Standard interpretability tools, such as Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs), are ill-equipped for NCO, whose decisions are dynamic, state-dependent, and lack proper concept vocabulary definition. To close this gap, we introduce Evolving Programmatic Bottlenecks (EPB), to our knowledge, the first framework for interpreting NCO policies by distilling black-box NCO models into human-readable program portfolios. EPB employs an LLM to autonomously evolve a bank of programs, where each program's per-step action distribution serves as the bottleneck. EPB works through an iterative framework: Block I fixes program bank capacity and introduces a hybrid textual-numerical gradient descent scheme that couples numerical gradients for student router updates and textual gradients for LLM-based program revision; Block II dynamically adapts bank capacity via fault-targeted expansion and redundancy pruning. Extensive experiments demonstrate EPB's effectiveness and broad applicability, where the distilled program portfolios largely match original performance. EPB also reveals that NCO behavior shifts across optimization stages and can be approximated as a composition of classic heuristic variants. Our work advances interpretable NCO and establishes EPB as a promising tool for interpreting sequential decision-making models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Evolving Programmatic Bottlenecks (EPB), a framework to enhance the interpretability of Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO) by distilling black-box NCO policies into human-readable program portfolios.
AI.motivation: There is a significant lack of interpretability in Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO), as traditional interpretability tools are unsuitable for dynamic and state-dependent decisions without clear concept vocabularies.
AI.method: The authors propose EPB, which uses a two-block iterative process: (1) Fixing program bank capacity with a hybrid gradient descent method combining numerical and textual gradients for evolving programs; (2) Dynamically adapting bank capacity by expanding or pruning the program bank based on faults and redundancies.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that EPB effectively distills NCO models, retains comparable performance to the original model, and provides insights into NCO behavior, including shifts during optimization stages.
AI.conclusion: EPB establishes itself as a powerful tool for interpreting sequential decision-making models, demonstrating that NCO behavior can be approximated by a composition of classical heuristic approaches.

title: From 50K to 8.2 Million in 24 Hours: Vozinha's Algorithmic Consecration and the Multilingual Making of World Cup Visibility
comment: 11 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables; v0.1 pilot preprint. Dataset and evidence package available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20722235
summary: We present a multilingual computational discourse analysis of how language constructed the algorithmic consecration of Vozinha, the 40-year-old Cape Verde goalkeeper, after Spain 0-0 Cape Verde at the 2026 FIFA World Cup. The study contributes a multilingual corpus in Portuguese, Spanish, English, and French; a nine-frame narrative taxonomy with cue-based frame annotation; a reproducible annotation pipeline combining LLM-assisted suggestion with human validation; and an analysis of cross-lingual narrative diffusion across discourse phases. We treat the platform follower count itself, narrated as "50k to 8M", as a linguistic object: a circulating and narratable proof of visibility rather than a mere measurement. The follower-growth timeline is used only as contextual metadata: we reconstruct a conservative phase structure, not a continuous API-native series, and type every datapoint by value class, confidence, and evidence type. The only exact primary scraper anchor is 8,235,652 followers at 2026-06-16 15:47 UTC; all other figures are reported as estimated ranges or thresholds, including an estimated pre-match baseline of 45k-56k. Findings suggest that distinct languages carried distinct frames: Portuguese mobilization, Spanish crisis, English nation-making, and a shared platform-metric spectacle through which peripheral athletic performance became globally visible. As a v0.1 pilot, the paper releases the corpus schema, frame taxonomy, annotation guidelines, hashed visual-evidence log, and typed timeline, while flagging full double annotation and inter-annotator agreement as planned work.

AI.tldr: This paper examines multilingual narrative and discourse around Cape Verde goalkeeper Vozinha's 2026 FIFA World Cup performance using computational analysis.
AI.motivation: To understand how language and discourse in multiple languages contribute to cross-lingual narratives and algorithmic visibility during global events.
AI.method: The study builds a multilingual corpus, develops a narrative taxonomy, uses an annotation pipeline with LLM-assisted suggestions and human validation, and analyzes narrative diffusion. It treats social media follower count as a linguistic object and classifies data by type, confidence, and evidence.
AI.result: Key findings reveal distinct narrative frames defined by language (e.g., mobilization in Portuguese, crisis in Spanish, nation-making in English) and shared themes like platform-metric spectacle around a peripheral athlete’s global visibility.
AI.conclusion: The pilot study introduces new methodologies, resources (e.g., corpus, taxonomy, guidelines), and insights into narrative dynamics across languages while acknowledging the need for further validation like inter-annotator agreement.

title: Beyond Static Endpoints: Tool Programs as an Interface for Flexible Agentic Web Services
summary: In the agentic web era, LLM-based agents increasingly invoke web services as tools, yet most interfaces remain \emph{static endpoints} that poorly express long-horizon workflows with loops, conditionals, joins, and retries. We present ToolPro, which represents an agent's tool intent as an \emph{executable tool program} that compactly encodes multi-step service interactions with explicit effect types. ToolPro combines constraint-guided program construction, effect-aware replay for exactly-once state-modifying calls, and a profile-driven policy that decides when program execution outperforms stepwise calling. We instantiate ToolPro over MCP-style services with WebAssembly sandboxing and evaluate it on diverse workflows of real-world applications. ToolPro reduces end-to-end latency by up to 53.4\% and client-side traffic by up to 96.1\%, with larger gains under higher network latency and workflow complexity.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ToolPro, a system for improving LLM-based agents' multi-step interactions with web services, enabling efficient workflows.
AI.motivation: Existing web service interfaces are static and insufficient for expressing complex workflows like loops or conditionals in the context of LLM-based agents.
AI.method: ToolPro encodes service interactions as executable tool programs, employs constraint-guided program design, uses effect-aware replay for tasks, and implements a profile-driven execution policy.
AI.result: ToolPro demonstrated significant gains in reducing latency by up to 53.4% and client-side traffic by up to 96.1%, particularly in high-latency and complex workflow scenarios.
AI.conclusion: ToolPro effectively improves the efficiency of LLM-based agents handling complex workflows, showcasing its utility for web-based service interactions.

title: CSWinUNETR: Segmentation of Thin Anatomical Structures in Medical Images
summary: Accurate segmentation of thin, tortuous anatomical structures, such as retinal vessels, cerebral vasculature, and facial wrinkles, remains challenging due to low contrast, frequent discontinuities, and severe class imbalance. Although recent convolutional and Transformer-based models have improved performance, they often yield fragmented predictions and fail to recover fine branches. We propose CSWinUNETR, a general-purpose backbone for 2D and 3D thin-structure segmentation. It employs cross-shaped stripe self-attention to model long-range principal-axis context and incorporates cyclic shifts to enhance information exchange across stripes. To better preserve fine-grained details, we further introduce a detail-enhanced multi-scale self-attention module that aggregates contextual features from multi-resolution representations. In addition, we propose sparse-control dynamic snake convolution, which reconstructs reliable dense curvilinear kernels from sparsely predicted control points to better follow tortuous geometry. Extensive experiments on four benchmarks across ophthalmology, neurovascular imaging, and dermatology demonstrate that CSWinUNETR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods without task-specific post-processing or topology-aware losses. The code is available at https://github.com/labhai/CSWinUNETR.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces CSWinUNETR, a novel model specialized for segmenting thin and complex anatomical structures, overcoming challenges like low contrast and discontinuities. It outperforms existing models across various medical imaging benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in segmenting fine and tortuous anatomical structures (e.g., retinal vessels, wrinkles), which are prone to fragmented predictions due to low contrast and class imbalance.
AI.method: The paper presents CSWinUNETR, which combines cross-shaped stripe self-attention, cyclic shifts, and a detail-enhanced multi-scale self-attention module for improved feature aggregation. It also introduces sparse-control dynamic snake convolution to better map tortuous geometries.
AI.result: CSWinUNETR achieves superior segmentation performance compared to state-of-the-art models across four medical-imaging-themed benchmarks in ophthalmology, neurovascular imaging, and dermatology.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CSWinUNETR offers a task-agnostic, high-performance solution for segmenting thin anatomical structures with improved accuracy and usability, verified by diverse and extensive benchmarks.

title: Comparative Study on Agility, Efficiency, and Impact Absorption of Bipedal Robots with Active Toes
summary: Human legs exhibit high efficiency, agility, and impact absorption, with toes playing a crucial role in these capabilities. While many attempts have been made to implement human-like toes in robots, they have not fully replicated human characteristics nor rigorously validated their benefits. We propose a 14-DOF biped robot emulating human toes' lightweight, high-torque, robust nature. To quantitatively analyze the effectiveness of the active toes in terms of agility, efficiency, and impact absorption, we developed a high-fidelity simulation training environment that reflects actual actuators with coupled transmissions and accurate power consumption. To ensure a fair comparison between configurations with and without active toes, we designed a minimal RL reward function and applied an identical training procedure to both. The simulation results indicate that, at 1.33 m/s walking, the toe-equipped robot reduced CoT by 17.5% and heel-strike GRF by 5.0% compared with the toe-ablation configuration. On the agility test, average and maximum path deviation decreased by 25.0% and 34.0%, respectively.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a 14-DOF biped robot with human-like toes, showing through simulations that these toes improve agility, efficiency, and impact absorption.
AI.motivation: To emulate and validate the benefits of human-like toe functionality in robots for improved efficiency, agility, and impact absorption.
AI.method: Developed a high-fidelity simulation environment with precise actuator modeling and used reinforcement learning to compare toe-equipped robots versus those without toes.
AI.result: Simulated toe-equipped robots achieved a 17.5% reduction in CoT, 5.0% decrease in heel-strike GRF, and improved agility with 25.0% and 34.0% reductions in average and maximum path deviations, respectively.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating human-like toes in biped robotic designs can significantly enhance performance metrics such as efficiency, agility, and impact absorption in simulated environments.

title: Spectral Retrieval-Augmented Time-Series Forecasting
summary: Time series forecasting leverages historical patterns to predict future values, but traditional methods face challenges when dealing with complex, non-stationary patterns that are difficult to memorize during training. Retrieval-augmented approaches have emerged as promising solutions by retrieving similar historical patterns to enhance predictions. However, existing retrieval methods suffer from two fundamental limitations: spectral blindness, which overlooks critical frequency-domain characteristics that capture underlying periodic structures, and temporal recency, which treats all historical data equally without emphasizing recent, more relevant patterns. In this paper, we propose SpecReTF, a novel retrieval method that addresses these issues by converting time series into windowed frequency representations, measuring similarity with a combined metric that captures both amplitude and phase information. To balance recency and historical context, we apply an exponential moving average weighting scheme that emphasizes recent windows. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that SpecReTF outperforms time-domain retrieval methods, achieving superior forecasting accuracy across diverse, non-stationary time series.

AI.tldr: SpecReTF uses frequency domain representations and exponential moving averages to improve time series forecasting by addressing spectral blindness and temporal recency issues.
AI.motivation: Traditional time series forecasting methods struggle with complex, non-stationary patterns. Retrieval-augmented approaches help but face challenges like neglecting frequency characteristics and failing to emphasize recent patterns.
AI.method: The proposed SpecReTF converts time series into frequency-domain representations, measures similarity using a combined amplitude-phase metric, and applies an exponential moving average for recency weighting.
AI.result: SpecReTF achieves superior forecasting accuracy compared to traditional time-domain retrieval methods in experiments on diverse benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: Addressing spectral and temporal limitations, SpecReTF enhances time series forecasting by leveraging frequency-domain insights and recency emphasis, making it effective for non-stationary data.

title: A Comparative Study of Pretrained Transformer Models for Quranic ASR: Speech Representations, Label Formats, and Dataset Composition
comment: 30 pages, 9 figures, 5 tables, Submitted to International Journal of Speech Technology
summary: Quran Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) aims to convert Quranic recitation into text, enabling applications such as aided memorisation tools and Quranic search engines. However, existing ASR models often exhibit high Word Error Rates (WER) on user-recited verses and lack full coverage of the Quranic corpus. This paper presents a systematic empirical study of domain-specific fine-tuning of pretrained Transformer-based models for Quranic ASR, using advanced speech feature extraction methods: Wav2Vec2.0, HuBERT, and XLS-R. These models apply self-supervised learning by masking portions of input audio and using Transformer architectures to learn context-aware speech features. The pretrained models are fine-tuned on a filtered Quranic dataset exceeding 870 hours of professional and user recitations. Through comprehensive ablation studies across feature extractors, output label formats, training strategies, and clip durations, we identify the key factors that affect transcription accuracy in this domain. Our best-performing configuration achieves a WER of 0.08 on the EveryAyah subset and 0.11 on the combined EveryAyah+Tarteel setting, representing roughly a five-percentage-point gain over the Citrinet baseline (WER = 0.163) while reducing combined-model training time from 140 hours to 40 hours. Arabic text without diacritics yields the best fine-tuning results, and Wav2Vec2-XLSR-53 provides the strongest overall representation. Future work includes improving dataset quality and developing phoneme-aware models to extract deeper speech feature representations for Tajweed-sensitive applications.

AI.tldr: The paper focuses on fine-tuning Transformer-based models for Quranic Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) to enhance transcription accuracy and reduce Word Error Rates (WER). Using methods like Wav2Vec2.0, HuBERT, and XLS-R, the researchers achieve a significant performance boost over the Citrinet baseline with reduced training time.
AI.motivation: Existing ASR models have high Word Error Rates (WER) on Quranic recitations and do not fully cover the Quranic corpus. The research aims to enhance Quranic ASR models for better transcription accuracy and usability, particularly for applications like Quranic memorization tools.
AI.method: The study fine-tunes pretrained Transformer-based models (Wav2Vec2.0, HuBERT, XLS-R) on over 870 hours of Quranic recitations. It conducts comprehensive ablation experiments on different aspects like feature extractors, training strategies, and output label formats.
AI.result: The best configuration achieves a WER of 0.08 on the EveryAyah subset and 0.11 on the combined dataset, improving accuracy and reducing training time from 140 hours to 40 hours compared to the Citrinet baseline.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuning Transformer-based models with specific audio processing techniques significantly improves Quranic ASR accuracy. The study highlights Arabic text without diacritics and Wav2Vec2-XLSR-53 as optimal for this task. Future work will focus on better datasets and Tajweed-sensitive phoneme-aware models.

title: SAGE-OPD: Selective Agent-Guided Intervention for Multi-Turn On-Policy Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) improves student models by training them on trajectories induced by their own policy, making it a promising approach for mitigating exposure bias in agent training. However, most OPD studies focus on single-turn settings, while realistic LLM agents interact with environments over multiple turns. In this regime, early errors can alter future observations and compound across the trajectory, and standard dense token-level OPD becomes brittle, as it may over-penalize semantically valid alternatives, reinforce local degeneracies such as repeated actions, and propagate unreliable teacher supervision on off-distribution histories. We propose SAGE-OPD, a verifier-free selective intervention framework specifically designed for multi-turn OPD. Instead of applying teacher supervision uniformly across all turns, SAGE-OPD first observes environment feedback and uses teacher judgment to decide whether each student response should be skipped or intervened on. To further address compounding errors, SAGE-OPD weights token-level distillation by teacher confidence, reducing the influence of uncertain teacher distributions on corrupted or ambiguous histories. Finally, SAGE-OPD applies loss normalization to preserve the overall loss scale of standard OPD while retaining selective turn-level weighting. Experiments on agent tasks show that SAGE-OPD consistently improves over baselines, achieving up to a 13.3% relative improvement in ALFWorld unseen success rate over standard OPD. Ablation studies further demonstrate that turn-level intervention, teacher confidence weighting, and loss normalization provide complementary benefits. Our results suggest that effective multi-turn OPD should remain on-policy, but teacher supervision should be selectively allocated to turns where intervention is necessary and reliable.

AI.tldr: SAGE-OPD introduces a selective, confidence-weighted distillation framework to improve multi-turn on-policy distillation (OPD) in large language models, addressing issues like exposure bias and compounding errors.
AI.motivation: To address the exposure bias and compounding errors that hinder the effectiveness of OPD in multi-turn settings with LLMs.
AI.method: SAGE-OPD incorporates selective teacher interventions based on environment feedback, weights distillation by teacher confidence to reduce unreliable supervision, and normalizes loss to retain overall OPD consistency in multi-turn tasks.
AI.result: SAGE-OPD outperforms standard OPD methods, achieving significant improvements (e.g., 13.3% in ALFWorld unseen success rate) and proving the advantages of selective supervision and weighting strategies.
AI.conclusion: SAGE-OPD highlights the importance of selective and confidence-driven teacher interventions in multi-turn OPD, suggesting that interventions should focus only on necessary and reliable cases for better multi-turn task outcomes.

title: When Lower Privileges Suffice: Investigating Over-Privileged Tool Selection in LLM Agents
summary: As LLM agents increasingly select tools autonomously, their choices among tools with different privileges become safety-relevant. However, prior tool-selection studies focus on safety-agnostic metadata preferences, leaving privilege-sensitive choices underexplored. To address this gap, we study over-privileged tool selection, in which an agent selects or escalates to a higher-privilege tool despite a sufficient lower-privilege alternative. We introduce ToolPrivBench to evaluate whether agents choose higher-privilege tools despite sufficient lower-privilege alternatives, measuring both initial selection and escalation after transient tool failures. Across eight domains and five recurring risk patterns, we find that over-privileged tool selection is common among mainstream LLM agents and is further amplified by transient failures. We further find that general safety alignment does not reliably transfer to least-privilege tool choice, while prompt-level controls provide only limited mitigation under transient failures. We therefore introduce a privilege-aware post-training defense that teaches agents to prefer sufficient lower-privilege tools and escalate only when necessary. Our mitigation experiments show that this defense substantially reduces unnecessary high-privilege tool use while preserving general capabilities.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the safety issue of LLM agents selecting over-privileged tools when lower-privilege alternatives suffice and proposes a defense to reduce this behavior.
AI.motivation: To address the safety concern of LLM agents selecting higher-privilege tools, which can pose risks, despite the availability of sufficient lower-privilege alternatives.
AI.method: The authors introduce ToolPrivBench to evaluate tool-selection behavior, identify risk patterns, and develop a privilege-aware post-training defense to guide agents toward least-privilege options.
AI.result: The study reveals that mainstream LLM agents often select over-privileged tools, especially during transient failures, but the proposed post-training defense significantly reduces such behavior.
AI.conclusion: Privilege-aware training can improve safety by encouraging LLM agents to select lower-privilege tools when appropriate, mitigating unnecessary risk while maintaining general performance.

title: 3D-PLOT-LLM: Part-Level Object Tokens for 3D Large Language Models
summary: 3D multimodal large language models (3D MLLMs) describe a 3D object as a whole but cannot address, name, or reason about its parts. Prior part-aware attempts add segmentation decoders, heavier 3D encoders, or bounding-box grammars at substantial parameter cost. We take a fundamentally different path: we reorganize the input token stream so that parts become directly addressable through the LLM's own vocabulary. Our model, 3D-PLOT-LLM, partitions the frozen point encoder's patches into K locally coherent regions and inserts, before each region's patch tokens, a learnable per-region marker and a reserved vocabulary token <part_k>; a Marker-Space Refinement (MSR) module then conditions each marker on its region's spatial statistics and adjacency neighbors. The model thus cites parts in its output and follows prompts that refer to parts by token, a capability absent from prior object-level 3D MLLMs. To probe this interface, we construct PartVerse-QA, a vocabulary-level part-QA benchmark adapted from PartVerse mesh annotations (77K training pairs and 588 held-out queries on disjoint object splits), on which 3D-PLOT-LLM reaches caption-to-slots Jaccard 0.459 and Exact-match 13.78%, with a slot-to-caption GPT-4o judge of 44.68. On the 3DCoMPaT-GrIn part-aware grounded description benchmark, 3D-PLOT-LLM outperforms PointLLM, Kestrel, PARIS3D, and SegPoint on every text-output metric, and ShapeLLM on 3 of 4, with up to +3.03 GPT-4o judge over PointLLM. On Objaverse whole-object captioning, adding PartVerse-QA at Stage 2 yields +0.65 SBERT and +1.85 GPT-4o over PointLLM, and tops PointLLM-PiSA on 4 of 5 traditional metrics (SBERT, SimCSE, BLEU-1, METEOR) despite targeting a different (part-grounded) objective. All with under 1M new trainable parameters on a frozen point encoder, an order of magnitude below prior part-aware 3D MLLMs, and no segmentation decoder or bounding-box head.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces 3D-PLOT-LLM, a 3D multimodal large language model with part-awareness that reorganizes input streams to address and reason about object parts without adding significant parameter costs.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D multimodal large language models lack the ability to address or reason about parts of a 3D object. Previous attempts to incorporate part-awareness have led to increased parameter costs due to additional components like segmentation decoders or bounding-box grammars.
AI.method: The authors propose 3D-PLOT-LLM, which partitions 3D objects into locally coherent regions and uses learnable markers and reserved tokens to represent these parts.
